rationale_for_baseline_tier: The patient has active bronchospasm with respiratory distress and hypoxia, plus ongoing high-volume secretory diarrhoea with dehydration risk and haemodynamic instability, on a background of probable carcinoid heart disease. The clinician's disposition was urgent emergency-department presentation for respiratory support and IV fluids, with an explicit instruction to call 000 if breathing became unmanageable or consciousness deteriorated. This is ED-level acuity now (T4 — attend ED within the hour, partner transport) with a mandatory T5 (call 000) edge on deterioration. Active respiratory distress is a patient-reportable time-critical feature, so T4 is appropriate rather than a T3 ceiling. Adrenaline must be avoided as it can precipitate carcinoid crisis.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Breathing becomes unmanageable, lips or face turn blue, or the patient becomes confused or drowsy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately — call 000 the moment any of these appear, including during transit
escalation_edges.rationale: These signal respiratory failure or hypoxic brain involvement — an emergency-ambulance response is required within minutes.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If her breathing becomes too hard to manage, her lips turn blue, or she becomes confused or drowsy, pull over safely and call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fainting, collapse, severe dizziness on standing, or inability to keep any fluids down with ongoing diarrhoea
escalation_edges.rationale: Indicates hypovolaemic/haemodynamic collapse or a carcinoid-crisis surge needing emergency resuscitation and IV therapy.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she faints, collapses, feels severely dizzy when standing, or cannot keep any fluids down, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Baseline disposition — active respiratory distress with ongoing secretory diarrhoea and vasomotor instability
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Now — proceed to the emergency department within the hour
escalation_edges.rationale: The current picture already warrants emergency-department assessment for respiratory support, IV fluids, electrolyte correction and specialist workup; do not wait for routine hours.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Head to the emergency department now — tonight, not in the morning. Take small sips of rehydration fluid on the way and avoid any alcohol.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Probable carcinoid heart disease (right-heart involvement) and carcinoid-crisis potential
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises the stakes of the acute episode and makes adrenaline dangerous; reinforces ED-level escalation and specialist (cardiology/endocrine/oncology) follow-up.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Partner present and able to assist and transport
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Enables safe partner-assisted transport to ED for T4, with 000 as the immediate fallback if deterioration occurs during transit.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Go to the emergency department now, tonight — this needs oxygen/breathing support, fluids into a vein and blood tests that can only be done in hospital. Take small, frequent sips of rehydration fluid on the way and avoid all alcohol. If her breathing becomes unmanageable, her lips turn blue, or she becomes confused or drowsy, call 000 for an ambulance immediately.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Attend the nearest emergency department now (partner to drive); call 000 if any deterioration
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: This is an out-of-hours emergency — the ED and 000 are available 24 hours; do not wait for a routine appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the ED with the history of recurrent episodic flushing, secretory diarrhoea, new bronchospasm, right-heart signs and weight loss, and the concern for a neuroendocrine (carcinoid) cause so that urinary 5-HIAA, chromogranin A, echocardiography and imaging can be arranged. GP review within 72 hours of discharge.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the diarrhoea as simple gastroenteritis or the wheeze as mild asthma and advising home management or routine/urgent GP review, missing the active respiratory distress and systemic instability.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Progressive respiratory failure, hypovolaemic collapse or carcinoid crisis at home without emergency support.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Directing to urgent same-day GP/urgent care instead of the emergency department despite active respiratory distress and ongoing fluid loss.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an ambulance immediately when partner-assisted ED transport is appropriate — safe but slightly over-triaged; acceptable given the deterioration risk.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The presentation strongly mimics anaphylaxis and asthma, and the underlying neuroendocrine cause cannot be confirmed via telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Exclude anaphylaxis as the immediate threat, avoid adrenaline given the atypical recurrent pattern, keep a broad differential, and escalate to the emergency department for stabilisation and specialist workup.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Committing to a single mimic — giving adrenaline for presumed anaphylaxis (risking carcinoid crisis), or labelling it asthma/gastroenteritis and under-escalating.
